text: The surgeon begins dissection over the sacral promontory, carefully identifying and cleaning the periosteum in preparation for suture pexy. The iliac veins are visible crossing the area, and the sacral bone is exposed.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection in the retrorectal space, frequently readjusting due to the excessive redundancy of the tissue.

The surgeon performs right-sided dissection in the retrorectal space, focusing on posterior and anterior areas while leaving the left side untouched.

The surgeon adjusts the robotic setup to two right arms and one left, positioning the camera centrally. Arm three, equipped with scissors, reaches into the pelvis without obstruction from the iliac vessels. The posterior dissection in the retrorectal space continues.

The surgeon navigates the retrorectal space towards the coccyx, addressing camera fogging issues caused by interstitial edema. The camera angle is adjusted to thirty degrees up to improve visibility in the deep pelvis.

The surgeon switches to anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum, carefully separating tissue layers in this area.

The surgeon encounters minor bleeding while dissecting in the rectovaginal septum. The dissection is continued to open up the septum further, assessing the depth of the current approach.

The surgeon completes the right-sided dissection, connecting it to the previously dissected posterior area in the pelvic region.

The surgeon completes the right-sided dissection, connecting it to the posterior dissection. Despite camera fogging due to interstitial edema, the rectum is lifted off the coccyx, revealing the anatomical structures in the deep pelvis.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the posterior and right side, followed by deep anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum. The vagina is visibly lifted off the rectum using a robotic arm, exposing the septum down to the perineal body.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection posteriorly along the right side, followed by deep anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum. Arm number four is used to elevate the vagina off the rectum, exposing the septum down to the perineal body.

The surgeon prepares to place rectopexy sutures after reaching the perineal floor. The cleaned periosteum is visible, allowing for secure suturing without risk of bleeding. The lateral stalks and peritoneum are pulled under tension, and large bites are taken using silk sutures.

The surgeon performs rectopexy sutures at the perineal floor, with the cleaned periosteum visible, allowing for secure suture placement without bleeding. The lateral stalks and peritoneum are pulled under tension as the surgeon takes large bites with the suture. The second right-sided suture is placed using tool silk.
The surgeon places the second right-sided suture in the rectopexy procedure, ensuring adequate repair by securing the tissue with precise bites.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal opening after completing the rectopexy, ensuring the small bowel is prevented from dropping into the space, which could lead to adhesions or an internal hernia.

The surgeon releases the uterus, achieving hemostasis at the needle puncture sites both anteriorly and posteriorly, before taking a final inspection of the surgical field.
The surgeon releases the uterus, achieving hemostasis at the needle entry points both anteriorly and posteriorly. A final inspection of the surgical site is performed before concluding the procedure.
